caveats: a: O*NET is an occupation-level source and does not measure the industry wage mix or AI exposure.
a: Ulta is a national-scale operator excluded from the lens and is more digitized than typical LMM firms.
a: The provided labor ratio is an ancestor 44-45 estimate with a 2024-wage/2022-receipts mismatch and IPS harmonization.

rho: Conventional self-checkout and warehouse automation are excluded unless AI is the enabling layer.

e: Ancillary salon or advisory activity may be reported inconsistently across establishments.

s5: Beauty M&A data often mix manufacturers, brands, distributors, salons and retailers.

q: Private-label, professional-only and salon-service mixes can retain materially different shares.
q: This is retention of implemented gross benefit, not merchandise gross margin.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

identity.title: Cosmetics, Beauty Supplies, and Perfume Retailers

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Consumers and beauty professionals buy replenishment and discretionary products at posted retail prices; qualifying operators add repeat advisory, loyalty, ordering, delivery, education, or in-store services, typically paid per transaction rather than under long-term contracts.
lens.deviation_from_default: This NAICS is primarily transactional merchandise retail, not outsourced service. To keep the screen coherent, it is narrowed to independent and regional operators where repeat replenishment or professional supply is paired with an ongoing advisory, fulfillment, loyalty, education, or in-store service relationship; pure one-off retail is excluded.
lens.excluded_activities: National chains, manufacturer-owned or captive distribution, pure product brands and manufacturers, salons without meaningful beauty retail, very small owner-operated shops below the EBITDA band, one-off kiosks, and passive or non-control vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US independent and regional specialty beauty retailers in the $1-10 million normalized-EBITDA band whose external customers repeatedly use product-replenishment, professional beauty-supply, consultation, loyalty, omnichannel fulfillment, or in-store beauty-service offerings.

a.bridge: No six-digit occupational table was located. O*NET describes retail-sales work rather than this beauty-retail lens, including needs discovery, recommendations, questions, transaction records, checkout, inventory monitoring, purchasing, demonstrations and delivery arrangements. Ulta, a much larger operator, demonstrates that personalized recommendations, virtual try-on, POS-driven replenishment and omnichannel fulfillment are already technologically feasible. I use those task lists to bound the not-yet-automated share for smaller operators; this likely overstates exposure where tactile demonstration and relationship selling dominate and understates it where small firms still perform reporting, marketing and planning manually.
caveats: O*NET is an occupation-level source and does not measure the industry wage mix or AI exposure.
Ulta is a national-scale operator excluded from the lens and is more digitized than typical LMM firms.
The provided labor ratio is an ancestor 44-45 estimate with a 2024-wage/2022-receipts mismatch and IPS harmonization.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted time study across independent and regional beauty retailers, by store and central function, showing hours spent on consultation, checkout, replenishment, merchandising, marketing, digital support, fulfillment and physical services, with current automation penetration.
a.rationale: AI can address product matching, routine advice, content and campaign creation, customer messaging, scheduling, demand forecasting, purchase ordering, e-commerce support, returns triage, training content and administrative reporting. Physical stocking, sample hygiene, visual merchandising, loss prevention, hands-on services and high-trust selling remain less substitutable. Existing recommendation and virtual-try-on tools are excluded from the current opportunity where already deployed.

d5.bridge: Circana measured one-year US beauty retail outcomes across prestige and mass channels, not a five-year constant-price service-basket forecast for specialty LMM operators. In 2025 prestige unit sales rose 4% and mass units rose 2%, while categories varied widely. I temper those rates toward roughly 2% annual real quantity growth and widen the range for macro sensitivity, trend reversals and specialty-channel share shifts; brand launches and mix can make unit growth an imperfect constant-quality measure.
caveats: Circana's mass channel extends well beyond this NAICS and its prestige channel includes non-specialty sellers.
One strong year is a weak basis for a five-year range.
Unit measures do not fully hold quality or pack size constant.
d5.change_evidence: Circana five-year unit forecasts and panel data segmented by specialty beauty store, mass, marketplace, brand-direct and professional supply, adjusted for pack size, product quality, store closures and same-customer replenishment.
d5.rationale: Beauty has repeat replenishment, product innovation and resilient self-care demand, but much of it is discretionary and trend-driven. Modest real growth is plausible, with faster fragrance and skincare pockets offset by makeup softness, economic trade-down and channel migration.

caveats: The frozen firm-count input is a declared gap and will be injected as MISSING, so this share cannot support a firm-count estimate.
Public-company examples show viable models but not the distribution of private-firm EBITDA.
Ancillary salon or advisory activity may be reported inconsistently across establishments.
e.change_evidence: Census or commercial owner-level data linking establishments to firms, normalized EBITDA, store count, customer mix, professional versus consumer revenue, repeat-purchase rate and service revenue.
e.rationale: The NAICS definition is merchandise retail, while the lens requires a repeat service relationship and $1-10 million EBITDA. Many establishments are single shops too small for the band; the largest specialty chains are above it. Eligibility is therefore limited to profitable regional groups, professional-supply operators and differentiated omnichannel or service-led concepts.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 44-45 ($651,293,900,629) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 44-45 ($6,850,896,411,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: ancestor 44-45. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1011 (margins.json[45]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Retail (General) EBITDA/Sales 10.11%); revenue/employee $430,267 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 44-45); DATA GAP: no SUSB size-class data for this code; n_band could not be computed — the research run must supply it with a shown chain, quality ESTIMATE
n.rationale: dataset n_band has no defensible value

o.bridge: Ulta reports that only 19% of loyalty members used both stores and digital channels in fiscal 2025, yet omnichannel members historically spent more than three times store-only members; it also operates personalized recommendations, virtual try-on, marketplace fulfillment and in-store salons. O*NET shows substantial human product advice and demonstration. These facts show coexistence of digital self-service and operator-led experience, not the exact future operator-required share. I assign more durability to professional supply and hands-on services and less to routine replenishment; the estimate may overstate LMM operator need if marketplaces and brand-direct channels absorb more demand.
caveats: A digital transaction still has an operator, but often not an operator of the lens's kind.
Hands-on salon services are ancillary within a retail NAICS and may be classified elsewhere when primary.
Category behavior differs sharply among fragrance, color cosmetics, hair, skincare and professional products.
o.change_evidence: Customer-level channel migration and journey data through 2031 showing which purchases require consultation, testing, professional credentials, education or immediate pickup, plus marketplace and brand-direct share by category and retailer size.
o.rationale: Routine replenishment, research, recommendations and checkout can be self-served online, and manufacturers or marketplaces can fulfill products without the current retailer. Tactile discovery, shade or texture matching, trusted professional advice, immediate availability, education and hands-on beauty services preserve a meaningful but not dominant role for the lens operator.

q.bridge: Ulta's filing measures a large chain's economics and competitive environment, not the retained share of an LMM operator's AI benefit. Its 39.1% fiscal-2025 gross margin, 26.6% SG&A ratio, coupon treatment, high brand concentration and description of aggressive cross-channel price competition imply that savings can be retained initially but leak through promotions, price matching, customer acquisition and service reinvestment. The proxy may understate differentiated private-label retention and overstate small firms' bargaining power with suppliers.
caveats: Ulta is larger, more diversified and more vertically advantaged than the lens population.
Private-label, professional-only and salon-service mixes can retain materially different shares.
This is retention of implemented gross benefit, not merchandise gross margin.
q.change_evidence: SKU- and store-level gross-margin cohorts before and after workflow deployment, tracking regular price, promotions, loyalty redemption, paid acquisition, vendor allowances, service reinvestment and competitor price gaps for two or more merchandise cycles.
q.rationale: Posted-price retail offers no automatic contractual pass-through, but products are highly comparable across specialty stores, mass merchants, marketplaces and brand-direct sites. Loyalty and professional relationships slow repricing; promotions, acquisition spend, service expectations and supplier power share much of the benefit over five years.

caveats: Large-chain digital capabilities may not be affordable or portable to LMM operators.
Revenue uplift is excluded; this primitive counts only implemented labor substitution or avoided hiring.
Conventional self-checkout and warehouse automation are excluded unless AI is the enabling layer.
rho.change_evidence: Twelve-month matched-store pilots from at least three regional beauty retailers reporting deployment cost, adoption, sales conversion, labor hours per transaction, inventory turns, customer complaints, returns and actual avoided hiring by workflow.
rho.rationale: Recommendation, marketing, service, forecast and support tools can be layered onto mature POS, CRM and e-commerce systems within five years, making implementation easier than in regulated clinical work. Realization is constrained by fragmented data, small IT teams, vendor integration, inventory complexity, associate adoption, privacy, brand voice, hallucinated product advice and the continued importance of human experience.

s5.bridge: BizBuySell tracks about 50,000 listed and recently sold US small businesses and reports 9,586 transactions across all sectors in 2025, but does not provide a beauty-retailer LMM denominator or a complete census of private deals. Beauty-supply stores are visibly marketed for sale on the platform. I use the broad small-business transfer market as a directional flow proxy and discount for the narrower EBITDA band, incomplete reporting, closures, asset sales and non-qualifying transactions.
caveats: Marketplace transactions are voluntarily reported and skew far smaller than the lens.
Listings demonstrate sale intent, not completed qualifying control transfers.
Beauty M&A data often mix manufacturers, brands, distributors, salons and retailers.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of closed transaction records from beauty-sector brokers, SBA 7(a) acquisition lenders, BizBuySell and CapIQ/PitchBook, deduplicated to US owner entity and tagged for EBITDA, store count, percentage acquired, buyer type, closure and asset-only sale.
s5.rationale: These are transferable stores with inventory, leases, vendor accounts and customer relationships, but a multi-site regional operator is harder to finance and integrate than the typical main-street listing. Channel disruption and owner dependence may cause closure or asset liquidation rather than a qualifying control transfer.

principal_driver: Repeat beauty replenishment plus rich product, loyalty and transaction data makes recommendation, marketing and inventory work readily AI-assistable.
principal_weakness: Most industry revenue is transactional product retail that can migrate to transparent online channels, leaving few service-led firms in the target EBITDA band.
